articles.title: Benzodiazepine Withdrawal Symptoms: What to Expect
articles.content:
Benzodiazepine withdrawal can produce a wide range of physical and psychological symptoms, especially when medications are stopped suddenly. Common symptoms include rebound anxiety, insomnia, irritability, muscle tension, headaches, and flu-like discomfort. In more severe cases, people may experience panic attacks, perceptual disturbances, and seizures.

Symptoms usually begin within 24–72 hours after the last dose for short-acting benzodiazepines, or several days later for longer-acting medications. The intensity and duration of withdrawal depend on factors such as dose, length of use, type of benzodiazepine, and co-occurring conditions.

Because of seizure risk and the potential for serious complications, medical supervision is strongly recommended. A gradual taper schedule, supportive medications, and monitoring in a detox or residential setting can significantly improve safety and comfort during benzodiazepine withdrawal.
articles.excerpt: Learn about common benzodiazepine withdrawal symptoms, when they start, and why medical supervision is essential for safety.
articles.author: Dr. Andrea Lee, MD

articles.title: Benzodiazepine Withdrawal Timeline: Day-by-Day Guide
articles.content:
Understanding the typical benzodiazepine withdrawal timeline can help you prepare for each stage and reduce anxiety about what is coming next. While every person is different, most people experience three broad phases of withdrawal: early, acute, and protracted.

In the early phase (days 1–4 for short-acting medications), people may notice rising anxiety, trouble sleeping, and physical tension. The acute phase (days 5–28) is often the most intense, with fluctuating symptoms such as panic, sensory sensitivity, gastrointestinal upset, and mood swings. For some, a protracted phase follows, in which milder symptoms such as fatigue, cognitive fog, and intermittent anxiety come and go for weeks or months.

A medically supervised taper, supportive psychotherapy, and lifestyle strategies—such as structured sleep routines and gentle exercise—can reduce the severity and duration of each phase. This guide offers a day-by-day overview, but your treatment team can tailor expectations to your specific medication and history.
articles.excerpt: Review a day-by-day benzodiazepine withdrawal timeline, from the first 72 hours through the protracted phase, and learn what supports can help.
articles.author: Karen Mitchell, LCSW

articles.title: Managing Benzodiazepine Withdrawal: Safe Tapering Strategies
articles.content:
The safest way to discontinue benzodiazepines is almost always through a slow, medically supervised taper. Abruptly stopping these medications can trigger severe withdrawal symptoms and medical complications.

A tapering plan typically involves switching to a longer-acting benzodiazepine when appropriate, then gradually reducing the dose over weeks or months. The pace of reduction is customized based on your starting dose, duration of use, co-occurring medical or mental health conditions, and previous withdrawal experiences.

In addition to medication adjustments, effective management includes cognitive-behavioral therapy for anxiety or insomnia, careful use of non-addictive sleep aids, and supportive coping strategies such as breathing exercises and relaxation techniques. Inpatient or residential detox programs offer 24/7 monitoring and can quickly address emerging complications.

Working closely with a prescriber and treatment team ensures that your taper is safe, flexible, and responsive to your symptoms.
articles.excerpt: Explore evidence-based strategies for safely tapering off benzodiazepines and reducing withdrawal risks.
articles.author: Dr. Michael Santos, DO

articles.title: Coping with Protracted Benzodiazepine Withdrawal Symptoms
articles.content:
For a subset of individuals, benzodiazepine withdrawal symptoms can persist or recur for months after the last dose. This experience, often called protracted withdrawal, may include waves of anxiety, insomnia, sensory hypersensitivity, and cognitive difficulties.

Although protracted symptoms can be discouraging, they typically decrease in intensity over time. Education, validation, and realistic expectations are critical. Many people benefit from structured psychotherapy, peer support groups, and careful management of lifestyle factors such as caffeine intake, sleep hygiene, and stress.

Clinicians may recommend non-addictive medications to target specific symptoms—such as antidepressants for mood or non-benzodiazepine agents for sleep—but these need to be used judiciously. Close follow-up with your medical and counseling team can help distinguish withdrawal-related symptoms from underlying mental health conditions.
articles.excerpt: Learn why some benzodiazepine withdrawal symptoms last longer, and practical strategies for coping with the protracted phase.
articles.author: Sarah Kim, PMHNP

articles.title: Benzodiazepine Detox vs. At-Home Withdrawal Management
articles.content:
When planning to come off benzodiazepines, one of the first decisions is whether to pursue medically supervised detox or attempt withdrawal at home. While some people can taper safely as outpatients, others face significant risks without structured support.

Supervised detox—either in an inpatient detox unit or residential program—offers continuous monitoring, seizure precautions, and rapid response to complications. It is strongly recommended for people taking high doses, those with a history of seizures or complicated withdrawal, or individuals with co-occurring alcohol or opioid use.

At-home withdrawal should only be considered under a prescriber’s guidance, with clear taper instructions and regular check-ins. Even then, many people find that the emotional and physical stress of withdrawal is easier to manage with a dedicated treatment team.

This article compares the benefits, risks, costs, and typical timelines of both approaches to help you make an informed decision with your clinician.
articles.excerpt: Compare medically supervised benzodiazepine detox with at-home withdrawal management, including safety, cost, and comfort considerations.
articles.author: Dr. Rachel Nguyen, MD

articles.title: Alcohol Detox: What Happens in the First 72 Hours
articles.content:
The first three days of alcohol detox are often the most medically significant. During this window, people may experience a rapid shift from mild withdrawal symptoms to potentially life-threatening complications.

Mild symptoms—such as tremor, sweating, anxiety, and insomnia—often begin within 6–12 hours after the last drink. Between 24 and 48 hours, some individuals develop hallucinations or withdrawal seizures. Delirium tremens, the most severe form of alcohol withdrawal, typically emerges between 48 and 72 hours and is characterized by confusion, severe autonomic instability, and vital sign changes.

In a supervised detox setting, clinicians monitor vital signs, provide IV fluids when needed, administer medications such as benzodiazepines to prevent seizures, and treat co-occurring conditions. Early assessment allows the team to stratify risk and decide whether inpatient medical detox, residential care, or closely monitored outpatient detox is most appropriate.
articles.excerpt: Understand the risks and protections involved in the first 72 hours of alcohol detox, including when medical detox is necessary.
articles.author: Dr. Jason Patel, MD

articles.title: Choosing Between Inpatient and Outpatient Alcohol Treatment
articles.content:
Selecting the right level of care is one of the most important treatment decisions you will make. Inpatient or residential programs provide 24/7 structure, medical support, and a recovery-focused environment. Outpatient programs, including intensive outpatient (IOP), allow you to live at home while attending therapy several days per week.

Key factors include withdrawal risk, medical complexity, home environment, and your ability to stay away from alcohol between sessions. People with severe withdrawal histories, multiple relapses, or unsafe living situations often do best in inpatient care. Those with stable housing, strong support, and milder symptoms may thrive in outpatient or IOP.

This guide outlines the pros and cons of each setting, including cost, insurance coverage, privacy, and typical daily schedules, to help you and your clinician choose a program that matches your needs.
articles.excerpt: Compare inpatient, residential, and outpatient alcohol treatment options, including who each level of care is best suited for.
articles.author: Emily Rogers, CADC

articles.title: Understanding Opioid Use Disorder and Treatment Options
articles.content:
Opioid use disorder (OUD) is a chronic medical condition that changes brain circuitry related to reward, motivation, and stress. Effective treatment typically combines medication for opioid use disorder (MOUD) with counseling and recovery support.

Medications such as buprenorphine, methadone, and naltrexone reduce cravings and withdrawal while improving safety and stability. These medications can be delivered in outpatient, intensive outpatient, and residential settings depending on your needs and stage of recovery.

Evening intensive outpatient programs are a common choice for people balancing work or school responsibilities. They offer multiple sessions per week, including group and individual therapy, relapse prevention skills, and medication management when indicated.
articles.excerpt: Learn how medications, therapy, and different levels of care work together to treat opioid use disorder.
articles.author: Dr. Luis Hernandez, MD

articles.title: Evening Intensive Outpatient Programs: How They Work
articles.content:
Evening intensive outpatient programs (IOP) are designed for people who need a high level of support but must maintain daytime responsibilities such as work, school, or caregiving.

Most evening IOP tracks meet three to four nights per week, typically between 6:00 PM and 9:00 PM. Sessions often include process groups, psychoeducation, skills training, and medication management check-ins when needed. Many opioid-specific tracks pair IOP with medication for opioid use disorder.

This format can be an excellent fit for individuals transitioning from residential care, as well as those starting treatment who have a stable home environment. Insurance plans commonly cover evening IOP at similar rates to daytime programs.
articles.excerpt: Review the structure, schedule, and benefits of evening intensive outpatient programs for substance use disorders.
articles.author: Mark Thompson, LMFT

articles.title: Family Support in Addiction Recovery: A Practical Guide
articles.content:
Family members often feel uncertain about how to help a loved one in treatment. Effective family support balances empathy and boundaries, offering encouragement without enabling substance use.

Many treatment centers provide structured family counseling, multi-family groups, and educational workshops. These services give relatives a chance to process their own emotions, learn communication skills, and coordinate with the clinical team.

Family involvement is associated with better engagement and outcomes, especially when visits and counseling sessions are built into the weekly schedule. Before admission, ask centers about visitation policies, weekend access, and how often family sessions occur.
articles.excerpt: Learn concrete ways families can support a loved one in treatment, and what to look for in family-friendly programs.
articles.author: Linda Perez, LMFT

articles.title: How to Prepare for a 30-Day Inpatient Rehab Stay
articles.content:
Preparing for a 30-day inpatient stay involves practical planning and emotional readiness. Most programs provide a packing list that limits valuables but encourages comfortable clothing and personal items.

Before admission, coordinate work leave or FMLA paperwork, arrange childcare or pet care, and identify trusted contacts who can manage urgent matters while you are in treatment. Emotionally, it can help to write down your goals for treatment and questions for your clinical team.

Understanding what a typical day looks like—detox check-ins, individual and group therapy, family sessions, and wellness activities—can reduce anxiety about the unknown.
articles.excerpt: From packing lists to work leave, learn how to prepare practically and emotionally for a 30-day inpatient rehab stay.
articles.author: Stephanie Cole, LCSW

articles.title: Understanding Insurance Coverage for Rehab
articles.content:
Health insurance can significantly reduce the cost of detox and rehab, but coverage rules can be confusing. Most commercial plans cover a range of services, including medical detox, residential treatment, partial hospitalization, and intensive outpatient programs.

Verification tools allow you to submit your member ID and date of birth so our admissions team can check benefits such as deductibles, co-pays, and out-of-pocket maximums. Many plans—like BlueCross BlueShield, Aetna, Cigna, and Medicaid—require preauthorization for residential stays longer than a set number of days.

As you compare 30- or 60-day residential programs, ask how your insurance applies and whether the center offers payment plans for remaining balances.
articles.excerpt: Learn how major insurers cover detox and rehab, and how to use our verification tool to estimate your costs.
articles.author: Admissions Team

articles.title: Women-Only Addiction Treatment: What to Expect
articles.content:
Women-only programs create a treatment environment tailored to the unique experiences of women in recovery. Groups often address topics such as trauma, parenting, relationships, and body image alongside core addiction work.

Residential women’s programs may offer gender-specific amenities, including secure housing, on-site childcare in some settings, and wellness activities like yoga and fitness groups. Many provide specialized tracks for pregnant women or mothers of young children.

When evaluating a women-only residential program, ask about program length—such as 30-, 45-, or 60-day options—clinical staff credentials, and access to wellness resources like gym facilities or walking trails.
articles.excerpt: Discover how women-only residential and outpatient programs tailor addiction treatment to women’s needs.
articles.author: Dr. Alisha Grant, PhD

articles.title: Navigating Anxiety and Alcohol Use: Dual Diagnosis Basics
articles.content:
Alcohol use and anxiety frequently occur together. Many people initially drink to reduce anxiety, only to find that alcohol worsens symptoms over time. This pattern is known as a dual diagnosis, or co-occurring disorder.

Effective treatment addresses both conditions at once. In practice, this may involve supervised alcohol detox, evidence-based therapies for anxiety, and non-addictive medications when appropriate. Programs that integrate mental health and substance use treatment—rather than treating them separately—tend to produce better outcomes.

If you are reaching out for help, mention both your alcohol use and anxiety symptoms. Our 24/7 helpline and callback request forms allow you to specify multiple concerns so the admissions team can match you with the right level of care.
articles.excerpt: Learn why anxiety and alcohol use so often overlap and how integrated treatment can help.
articles.author: Dr. Nina Wallace, MD

articles.title: What to Expect in a 45-Day Residential Program
articles.content:
Forty-five-day residential programs offer more time than standard 28–30 day stays, allowing deeper work on underlying issues and relapse prevention. The first week is often focused on detox stabilization and orientation. Weeks two through four typically emphasize core therapy, skills training, and family involvement.

The final phase of a 45-day stay is usually devoted to transition planning: building a relapse prevention plan, coordinating outpatient or intensive outpatient follow-up, and preparing family members for ongoing support. Women-only tracks may incorporate gender-specific groups, trauma-focused therapy, and wellness activities like gym access or yoga.

Understanding the general structure of a 45-day program can help you decide whether this length is a good fit for your goals and responsibilities.
articles.excerpt: See how a typical 45-day residential program is structured, from detox and therapy to family sessions and aftercare planning.
articles.author: James Miller, LPC

articles.title: Telehealth in Addiction Treatment: Pros and Cons
articles.content:
Telehealth has become an integral part of addiction treatment, particularly for counseling and medication management. Virtual visits increase access for people living far from treatment centers or those with transportation barriers.

However, telehealth is not appropriate for everyone. People with high medical risk during detox or unstable home environments may need in-person care. Insurance coverage and state licensing rules can also impact availability.

Hybrid models that combine in-person assessments with telehealth follow-ups offer a flexible approach for many patients.
articles.excerpt: Explore the advantages and limitations of telehealth for addiction treatment, including when in-person care is still essential.
articles.author: Clinical Team

articles.title: Video: Understanding Medical Detox
articles.content: In this short video, our medical director explains what happens during medical detox for alcohol, opioids, and benzodiazepines, including safety monitoring and comfort-focused care.
articles.excerpt: Watch a 6-minute overview of the medical detox process and what to expect.
articles.author: Video Team

articles.title: Podcast: Family Recovery Stories
articles.content: In this podcast episode, three families share their experiences supporting a loved one through detox, residential treatment, and aftercare, highlighting what helped most along the way.
articles.excerpt: Listen to families describe how they navigated treatment, visitation, and ongoing recovery support.
articles.author: Family Programs Team

assessment_questions.text: What is the primary substance you are most concerned about right now?
assessment_questions.help_text: Choose the substance that feels like the biggest concern today, even if you use more than one.
options: Not sure / I need help figuring this out

assessment_questions.text: How often do you typically use your primary substance?
assessment_questions.help_text: If your use varies, choose the option that best reflects the last 30 days.

assessment_questions.text: When you cut down or stop, how severe are your withdrawal symptoms?
assessment_questions.help_text: Consider symptoms like shakes, sweating, nausea, anxiety, trouble sleeping, or seizures.
options: 0 - I do not notice any withdrawal symptoms
5 - Moderate symptoms that are hard to ignore
7 - Severe symptoms or history of seizures / delirium tremens

assessment_questions.text: Have you ever been in treatment for substance use before?
assessment_questions.help_text: Include detox, residential, outpatient, or support groups.
options: No, this would be my first time in treatment
Yes, I’ve been in residential or inpatient treatment
Yes, I’ve been in outpatient or intensive outpatient treatment
Yes, I’ve attended support groups only (AA, NA, etc.)
Yes, I’ve tried multiple types of treatment

assessment_questions.text: Do you have a preference for the type of treatment setting?
assessment_questions.help_text: If you are not sure, we will suggest options based on your answers.

assessment_questions.text: Have you experienced any of the following mental health symptoms recently?
assessment_questions.help_text: Check all that apply, even if you are not formally diagnosed.
options: Hallucinations or seeing / hearing things others do not

assessment_questions.text: How stable and supportive is your current home environment?
assessment_questions.help_text: Consider safety, substance use in the home, and support from people you live with.
options: Somewhat supportive, but substances are occasionally present
Stressful or conflictual, with frequent substance use around me
Currently homeless or in very unstable housing

assessment_questions.text: Are you currently concerned about your safety related to substance use or mental health?
assessment_questions.help_text: This includes overdose risk, medical complications, or thoughts of self-harm.
options: Yes, I am at immediate risk and need help right away
Yes, I am concerned but not in immediate danger
No, I do not feel at risk right now

assessment_questions.text: How much support do you currently have from family, friends, or community?
assessment_questions.help_text: Think about people who encourage recovery and could support you during treatment.
options: Strong support (several people I can rely on)
Some support (one or two people I can lean on)
Limited support (people are inconsistent or only somewhat helpful)
No support (I feel mostly alone in this)

assessment_questions.text: When are you most available to attend treatment sessions?
assessment_questions.help_text: Your answer helps us match you with daytime or evening programs.

centers.name: San Francisco Recovery Center
centers.description: A full-service urban campus offering medical detox, residential treatment, intensive outpatient care, and robust family services in the heart of San Francisco.
centers.visitation_policy_text: Family and approved visitors are welcome Thursday through Sunday. Standard visiting hours are 2:00 PM–7:00 PM on weekdays and 12:00 PM–6:00 PM on Saturday and Sunday. Children are permitted during designated family hours. All visitors must check in at the front desk and follow confidentiality and safety guidelines.
centers.family_services_description: The center offers a weekly multi-family education group, a separate family support group, and at least one dedicated family counseling session per week for each residential patient. Additional sessions can be scheduled as clinically indicated.

centers.name: Los Angeles Hope & Wellness Center
centers.description: A metropolitan treatment center focused on detox, short-term residential care, and outpatient services for adults in downtown Los Angeles.
centers.visitation_policy_text: Due to space and security considerations, in-person visits are limited to Saturdays from 1:00 PM–5:00 PM for adult family members and approved support persons. Virtual visits via secure video are available by appointment during the week.
centers.family_services_description: The center offers a biweekly family education workshop and optional phone check-ins with the primary therapist. Individual family counseling sessions are available on a limited basis upon request.

centers.name: Orange County Renewal Center
centers.description: A campus-style facility in Orange County offering residential, detox, and day treatment programs with a strong emphasis on wellness and fitness.
centers.visitation_policy_text: Family visits are available on Saturdays and Sundays from 12:00 PM–4:00 PM, with additional evening visitation on Wednesdays by appointment. The center encourages regular family involvement and offers on-site family meals once a month.
centers.family_services_description: Residential patients receive one family counseling session per week and access to a weekly family support group. Educational workshops on communication and relapse prevention are offered monthly.

centers.name: Mountain View Recovery Center
centers.description: A Colorado-based center providing medical detox, residential treatment, and outpatient services with scenic mountain views and outdoor programming.
centers.visitation_policy_text: Visitation is offered on Sundays from 1:00 PM–5:00 PM for close family members and approved support persons. Virtual family sessions are encouraged for out-of-state relatives.
centers.family_services_description: The center provides a weekly virtual family education series, optional family counseling sessions, and coordination with local support groups for loved ones.

helplines.label: 24/7 Admissions & Crisis Helpline
helplines.description: Call anytime for immediate support, detox placement, and help choosing the right program. Licensed clinicians and admissions specialists are available 24 hours a day, 7 days a week.

helplines.label: Family Support & Education Line
helplines.description: Dedicated line for family members seeking guidance about helping a loved one, available 9 AM–8 PM Pacific Time, Monday through Saturday.

helplines.label: Outpatient & IOP Intake Line
helplines.description: Contact this line to schedule assessments for outpatient and evening intensive outpatient programs, available 8 AM–6 PM local time on weekdays.

insurance_providers.name: BlueCross BlueShield
insurance_providers.notes: Many BCBS plans cover medical detox, residential, and intensive outpatient levels of care with prior authorization for longer stays.

insurance_providers.name: Aetna
insurance_providers.notes: Typically requires utilization review for residential stays beyond 30 days; often supports step-down to IOP.

insurance_providers.name: Cigna
insurance_providers.notes: Covers a range of substance use and mental health services; telehealth options may be included.

insurance_providers.name: UnitedHealthcare
insurance_providers.notes: Preauthorization often required for inpatient rehab; many plans include behavioral health case managers.

insurance_providers.name: State Medicaid (varies by state)
insurance_providers.notes: Coverage for detox and residential treatment varies by state; our team can verify eligibility and available benefits.

support_group_sessions.title: Family Support Group – Coping with a Loved One’s Use
support_group_sessions.description: A psychoeducational and support-focused group for family members and close friends of adults in treatment. Topics include setting boundaries, communication skills, and self-care.
support_group_sessions.audience: Adult family members and significant others of current or recent patients

support_group_sessions.title: Relapse Prevention Skills Group
support_group_sessions.description: Patient-focused group covering triggers, cravings, and relapse prevention planning for individuals in outpatient or step-down care.
support_group_sessions.audience: Adult patients in outpatient or intensive outpatient programs

support_group_sessions.title: Open Community Recovery Support (Online)
support_group_sessions.description: A moderated, open-format online support group for anyone impacted by substance use, including individuals in recovery, family members, and community members.
support_group_sessions.audience: Adults seeking support for their own or a loved one’s substance use, no affiliation required

support_group_sessions.title: Weekend Family Education Workshop
support_group_sessions.description: A structured Saturday morning workshop covering addiction basics, stages of change, and how families can support recovery without enabling.
support_group_sessions.audience: Family members and support persons of current and former patients

support_group_sessions.title: Women’s Recovery & Family Connections (Hybrid)
support_group_sessions.description: A women-focused group exploring the impact of addiction on family systems, parenting, and relationships. Family members can join virtually for the final portion of the session.
support_group_sessions.audience: Women in treatment and their invited family members or partners
support_group_sessions.recurrence_pattern: Weekly on Thursdays at 6:00 PM (hybrid in-person/online)

support_group_sessions.title: Orange County Family Support Circle
support_group_sessions.description: Weekly family support group with facilitated discussion and psychoeducation for loved ones of patients in detox and residential programs.
support_group_sessions.audience: Family members of adults receiving care at Orange County Renewal Center

support_group_sessions.title: Daytime Recovery Skills Group
support_group_sessions.description: Core therapy group for residential patients focusing on coping skills, emotional regulation, and relapse prevention planning.
support_group_sessions.audience: Adults enrolled in residential or day treatment programs

support_group_sessions.title: Alumni Recovery Check-In Group
support_group_sessions.description: Weekly alumni group providing ongoing peer connection, accountability, and recovery support for graduates of any Harbor Recovery residential program.
support_group_sessions.audience: Program alumni who have completed any level of care

support_group_sessions.title: San Francisco Family & Friends Evening Group
support_group_sessions.description: An open-format group for family and friends to learn about addiction, treatment, and recovery while connecting with others in similar situations.
support_group_sessions.audience: Family members, partners, and close friends of people with substance use concerns

support_group_sessions.title: Morning Recovery Skills Group
support_group_sessions.description: Structured skills-based group for detox and residential patients, focusing on grounding techniques, distress tolerance, and craving management.

support_group_sessions.title: Weekend Alumni Support Group
support_group_sessions.description: Drop-in alumni meeting providing peer support, recovery check-ins, and discussions about maintaining sobriety in early and long-term recovery.
support_group_sessions.audience: Alumni and guests in active recovery

support_group_sessions.title: Mountain View Family Education & Support
support_group_sessions.description: A combination education and support group for families, covering how to respond to cravings, lapses, and communication challenges after discharge.
support_group_sessions.audience: Family members of current patients and recent alumni

support_group_sessions.title: Trauma-Informed Recovery Group
support_group_sessions.description: Therapist-led group integrating trauma-informed care principles with relapse prevention and grounding skills.
support_group_sessions.audience: Residential and outpatient patients with trauma histories

support_group_sessions.title: Nationwide Online Recovery Community Group
support_group_sessions.description: An open, online-only recovery group focused on building peer connection and sharing practical strategies for staying sober day-to-day.
support_group_sessions.audience: Adults in recovery across the U.S., including alumni and community members

support_group_sessions.title: Peer-Led Mindfulness & Recovery Circle
support_group_sessions.description: A peer-facilitated group integrating mindfulness practices, brief meditation, and discussion about using these tools in recovery.
support_group_sessions.audience: Current patients and alumni interested in mindfulness-based recovery tools

programs.name: 30-Day Inpatient Alcohol Detox & Recovery (Private Room)
programs.description: A 28–30 day medically supervised inpatient program focused on alcohol detox and early recovery. Patients stay in a private or semi-private room on a dedicated detox and stabilization unit before transitioning into core therapy and relapse-prevention work.
programs.schedule_summary: 24/7 medical monitoring with daily groups and individual therapy; medical detox in the first 3–7 days followed by structured inpatient programming.

programs.name: 60-Day Residential Dual Diagnosis Program (Value Track)
programs.description: A cost-conscious 60-day residential track for adults with alcohol use disorder and co-occurring anxiety or depression. Shared rooms, daily therapy, and integrated psychiatry visits are included.
programs.schedule_summary: Structured 7-day schedule with morning and afternoon groups, weekly psychiatry, and weekend family programming.

programs.name: 60-Day Residential Dual Diagnosis Program (Standard)
programs.description: A 60-day residential program with enhanced amenities, including optional private rooms, expanded wellness services, and intensive family involvement for adults with co-occurring mental health needs.
programs.schedule_summary: Daily evidence-based groups, twice-weekly individual therapy, weekly family sessions, and evening wellness activities.

programs.name: Evening Intensive Outpatient Program for Opioid Use Disorder
programs.description: A 6–10 week intensive outpatient program held on weekday evenings for adults with opioid use disorder, often combined with medication for opioid use disorder (MOUD).
programs.schedule_summary: Meets three evenings per week from 6:00–9:00 PM, including group therapy, psychoeducation, and medication management visits.

programs.name: Daytime Outpatient Program for Alcohol & Anxiety
programs.description: A flexible daytime outpatient program for individuals with mild to moderate alcohol use concerns and co-occurring anxiety disorders, focused on CBT, medication management, and relapse prevention.
programs.schedule_summary: Meets 1–3 mornings per week depending on clinical need; medication management visits scheduled separately.

programs.name: Los Angeles Evening Intensive Outpatient for Opioid Recovery
programs.description: A structured evening IOP track for adults with opioid use disorder who need at least three evening sessions per week while maintaining daytime responsibilities. Integrates MOUD, group therapy, and relapse-prevention skills.
programs.schedule_summary: Meets four evenings per week (Mon–Thu, 6:00–9:00 PM) with optional Saturday check-in group.

programs.name: Outpatient Opioid Recovery Group (Evenings)
programs.description: A lower-intensity outpatient option meeting several evenings per week for individuals stepping down from IOP or residential opioid treatment.
programs.schedule_summary: Meets three evenings per week from 6:30–8:30 PM with optional monthly family night.

programs.name: Daytime Outpatient Program for Alcohol Recovery
programs.description: A flexible daytime outpatient program for adults seeking help with alcohol use who can safely live at home, including CBT groups and individual counseling.
programs.schedule_summary: Meets two mornings per week from 9:00 AM–12:00 PM; evening family group available separately.

programs.name: 7–10 Day Medical Detox (Alcohol, Opioids, Benzodiazepines)
programs.description: A short-stay, medically managed detox unit for adults withdrawing from alcohol, opioids, benzodiazepines, or multiple substances, with transition planning into ongoing care.
programs.schedule_summary: 24/7 medical supervision with frequent nursing checks and daily medical provider rounding; groups offered as patients are medically able.

programs.name: 45-Day Women-Only Residential Program with Wellness & Fitness
programs.description: A women-only 40–45 day residential program emphasizing trauma-informed care, peer support, and access to a fully equipped gym and wellness activities.
programs.schedule_summary: Daily groups and individual therapy with evening wellness classes; family day offered twice per month.

programs.name: 30-Day Coed Residential Recovery Program
programs.description: A 28–32 day residential program for adults seeking a structured, supportive environment to begin recovery from alcohol and other substances.
programs.schedule_summary: Full daily schedule with morning and afternoon groups, evening community meetings, and weekend recreational activities.

programs.name: Orange County Evening Outpatient Track for Opioids
programs.description: An evening outpatient program focused on opioid recovery, ideal for individuals stepping down from residential or IOP who still benefit from three evening sessions weekly.
programs.schedule_summary: Meets Monday, Wednesday, and Friday evenings from 6:00–8:00 PM.

programs.name: 30-Day Residential Program for Alcohol & Anxiety
programs.description: A 30-day residential program on the Mountain View campus specializing in co-occurring alcohol use and anxiety disorders, with integrated psychiatry and evidence-based therapies.
programs.schedule_summary: Daily therapy schedule with outdoor experiential groups and weekly family video sessions.

programs.name: Evening Intensive Outpatient for Co-Occurring Disorders
programs.description: An evening IOP for adults with combined substance use and mental health conditions, offering three to four evening sessions weekly.
programs.schedule_summary: Meets Monday, Tuesday, and Thursday evenings from 5:30–8:30 PM with optional Wednesday skills group.

programs.name: 10–14 Day Benzodiazepine Medical Detox
programs.description: A medically supervised benzodiazepine detox program with individualized taper plans, seizure precautions, and 24/7 monitoring.
programs.schedule_summary: 24/7 inpatient-level care with gradual taper, vital sign monitoring, and supportive counseling.

programs.name: 60-Day Men’s Residential Recovery Program
programs.description: A men-only 60-day residential program focusing on addiction, trauma, and healthy masculinity, with a mix of process groups and skills training.
programs.schedule_summary: Structured weekdays with therapy groups and evening peer-led meetings; recreational outings on weekends.

program_reviews.title: Evening schedule made treatment possible
program_reviews.body: I work full-time and thought rehab wasn’t an option until I found this evening IOP. The groups were focused, staff understood opioid addiction, and they coordinated my Suboxone smoothly.

program_reviews.title: Felt truly understood here
program_reviews.body: I’ve tried other programs that treated me like a number. Here the counselors remembered details about my life and followed up when I was struggling with cravings after work.

program_reviews.title: Great program, parking can be tough
program_reviews.body: The IOP itself is excellent and really helped stabilize me. Only downside is downtown parking in the evenings, so plan extra time to get there.

program_reviews.title: Supportive staff and solid structure
program_reviews.body: Three hours a night sounded long, but the mix of process group, education, and check-ins made the time fly. I never felt judged for relapsing before coming back.

program_reviews.title: Helped me stay clean and keep my job
program_reviews.body: I was one write-up away from losing my job. Being able to go to IOP after work saved my career and gave me tools to stay off pills.

program_reviews.title: Strong opioid-focused group
program_reviews.body: Most of the group was dealing with opioids, which made it easier to relate. I wish there were a few more meditation or relaxation sessions, but overall it was very good.

program_reviews.title: Felt safe starting Suboxone here
program_reviews.body: The doctor and nurses explained every step of starting Suboxone. Having that plus group support in the evenings made detox much less scary.

program_reviews.title: They involved my family in a healthy way
program_reviews.body: The family nights helped my parents understand addiction without shaming me. That made a big difference in our relationship at home.

program_reviews.title: Practical tools I still use
program_reviews.body: Craving plans, coping cards, and grounding exercises from group are things I still use six months later. This program gave me real-life tools, not just lectures.

program_reviews.title: Flexible and respectful
program_reviews.body: They worked around my custody schedule and were understanding when I needed to step out to take kid-related calls. I always felt respected.

program_reviews.title: Intense but worth it
program_reviews.body: Four nights a week was a big commitment, but it kept me accountable. A couple of groups felt repetitive, but overall the structure helped me stay clean.

program_reviews.title: Best experience I’ve had in treatment
program_reviews.body: I’ve been to detox and rehab before, but this was the first time I really understood my addiction and how to manage triggers at night and on weekends.

program_reviews.title: Counselors who really care
program_reviews.body: The counselors checked in if I missed a day and helped me problem-solve instead of scolding me. That kept me coming back.

program_reviews.title: Good balance of structure and freedom
program_reviews.body: I could still work and see my kids, but I had somewhere to go every evening instead of using. The routine was exactly what I needed in early recovery.

program_reviews.title: Felt safe sharing my story
program_reviews.body: The group norms made it easy to open up, even about things I was ashamed of. Nobody laughed or minimized my pain.

program_reviews.title: Helped me transition from rehab
program_reviews.body: I came here right after a 30-day residential program. The IOP gave me a soft landing and support while I figured out life back in the real world.

program_reviews.title: Great for people with evening cravings
program_reviews.body: My hardest time was always late evening. Being in group from 6�9 made it much easier to get through that window without using.

program_reviews.title: Very helpful, room for more alumni support
program_reviews.body: The program was strong overall. I just wish there were more formal alumni events after graduation to stay connected.

program_reviews.title: Clear plan and expectations
program_reviews.body: From day one they explained what to expect, how long I’d be in IOP, and what step-down would look like. That clarity reduced a lot of my anxiety.

program_reviews.title: Life-changing experience
program_reviews.body: I walked in feeling hopeless and walked out with nearly 90 days clean and a plan. The evening format and staff here changed my life.
